title: Angel food cake
subtitle: The American egg-white cake: pure white, tall, and impossibly light
excerpt: A fatless American cake made entirely from egg whites, flour, and sugar, baked in a tube tin and cooled upside-down. Snow-white crumb, a faint vanilla sweetness, and a chew unlike anything made with butter.
sourceNotes: Angel food cake emerged in the American Midwest in the 1870s and appears in its modern form in Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896), Project Gutenberg, which gives the recipe with a tube pan and inverted cooling method. The dish uses only egg whites, leaving the yolks available for other preparations; the name references the lightness of the crumb.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in slices. Thaw at room temperature for two hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps at room temperature for two days, loosely covered. Refrigeration dries it out.

glossaryTerms.definition: A sponge cake made with no butter or oil, relying entirely on whisked eggs for its structure and lift.

content.text: Angel food cake is the most extreme example of a

: no egg yolks, no butter, no oil. The entire structure comes from a meringue of egg whites, into which a small amount of flour and sugar is folded. The result is pure white, very tall, with a moist, elastic chew that is unlike anything made with fat. It can take the weight of a thick fruit sauce without becoming soggy.

The technique is straightforward: whisk whites to stiff peaks, fold in sifted flour and sugar as gently as possible, bake in an ungreased tube tin, and cool upside-down. The cooling step is critical — the structure is too fragile to support its own weight when warm, and inverting it allows it to set under gravity without collapsing.

attrs.prepNote: divided: 150 g whisked in, 100 g sifted with the flour

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Do not grease the tube tin. Whisk {{egg-whites}} with {{cream-of-tartar}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} on medium speed until foamy, about 2 minutes. Increase speed and add the first 150 g of {{caster-sugar}} a tablespoon at a time over 3–4 minutes, whisking to stiff, glossy peaks. Add {{vanilla-extract}} and whisk briefly to combine.

Sift {{plain-flour}} with the remaining 100 g of {{caster-sugar}} together three times. Sift one third over the whites and fold with a large metal spoon using a cutting and lifting motion. Repeat with the remaining two additions. Work gently; losing as little volume as possible is the aim.

Spoon the batter into the ungreased tube tin and smooth the surface. Bake for 35–40 minutes until the top is deep golden-brown and a skewer comes out clean. Remove from the oven and immediately invert onto a wire rack. The tin will sit on its central tube. Leave for at least 60 minutes until fully cool before running a palette knife around the edges and unmoulding.
The 10 yolks left over can be used for crème brûlée, pasta, or mayonnaise. Do not waste them.
Serve with whipped cream and macerated strawberries, or with a simple lemon curd. The neutral sweetness of the cake pairs well with anything sharp or creamy.
Day-old angel food cake is excellent sliced and griddled in a dry pan until lightly caramelised on both surfaces.
